questionText: A student writes: "If a shape is a square, then it has four equal sides." Write down the converse of this statement, and decide whether the converse is true, giving a reason.

steps.workingLatex: P:\ \text{the shape is a square}, \qquad Q:\ \text{the shape has four equal sides}
steps.explanation: The original statement runs from $P$ to $Q$. Isolating each part is the first move in building its converse.

steps.description: Recall the rule for forming a converse.
steps.explanation: The converse swaps hypothesis and conclusion, reversing the arrow. It asks the reverse question and may have a different truth value from the original.

steps.workingLatex: \text{If a shape has four equal sides, then it is a square}
steps.explanation: Interchanging $P$ and $Q$ produces the new statement. Phrasing it plainly makes it easy to challenge with a specific shape.

steps.description: Plan how to test the converse.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Seek a shape with four equal sides that is not a square}
steps.explanation: One counterexample disproves a general geometric claim. So we look for a four-equal-sided shape whose other properties stop it being a square.
A rhombus has four equal sides, so it satisfies the converse's hypothesis, but its angles are not all right angles. A square additionally requires four right angles.

steps.description: Confirm the rhombus breaks the conclusion.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Rhombus has four equal sides but is not a square}
steps.explanation: Because the rhombus meets the hypothesis yet fails to be a square, it is a valid counterexample to the converse.
Four equal sides alone do not force a shape to be a square, since the angles can differ from $90^\circ$. The converse is therefore false.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Converse: "If a shape has four equal sides, then it is a square." This is false; a rhombus has four equal sides but is not a square, because its angles need not be $90^\circ$.
